content:
# Exodus Chapter 19 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44r–44v (PDF pp. 84–85); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 60–61 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_019_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_019_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Israel Arrives at Sinai (vv. 1–2)

**1** In the third month [**bachodesh hashlishi**] after the going out [**letset**] of the sons of Israel [**beney-Yisra'el**] from the land of Egypt [**me'erets Mitsrayim**], on this day [**bayyom hazzeh**], they came [**ba'u**] to the wilderness of Sinai [**midbar Sinay**].

**2** And they set out [**vayyis'u**] from Rephidim [**meRefidim**] and came [**vayyavo'u**] to the wilderness of Sinai [**midbar Sinay**], and they encamped [**vayyachanu**] in the wilderness [**bamidbar**]; and Israel encamped [**vayyichan-sham Yisra'el**] there before the mountain [**neged hahar**].

---

## The Eagle-Wing Speech: Covenant Proposal (vv. 3–6)

**3** And Moses went up [**uMosheh alah**] to God [**el-ha'Elohim**], and the LORD called [**vayyiqra elav YHWH**] to him from the mountain [**min-hahar**], saying [**le'mor**], "Thus shall you say [**koh tomar**] to the house of Jacob [**leveyt Ya'aqov**] and declare [**vetagged**] to the sons of Israel [**livney Yisra'el**]:

**4** 'You yourselves have seen [**attem re'item**] what I did [**asher asiti**] to Egypt [**leMitsrayim**], and I bore you [**va'essa etkhem**] on wings of eagles [**al-kanfey nesharim**], and I brought you [**va'avi etkhem**] to Myself [**elay**].

**5** And now [**ve'attah**], if you will indeed listen to [**im-shamoa tishme'u**] My voice [**beqoli**] and keep [**ushmartem**] My covenant [**et-beriti**], then you shall be to Me [**vihyitem li**] a treasured possession [**segullah**] from among all the peoples [**mikkol-ha'ammim**], for all the earth is Mine [**ki-li kol-ha'arets**].

**6** And you — you shall be to Me [**ve'attem tihyu-li**] a kingdom of priests [**mamlechet kohanim**] and a holy nation [**vegoy qadosh**].' These are the words [**elleh haddevarim**] that you shall speak [**asher tedabber**] to the sons of Israel [**el-beney Yisra'el**]."

---

## The People's Response and Consecration (vv. 7–9)

**7** And Moses came [**vayyavo Mosheh**] and called [**vayyiqra**] for the elders of the people [**leziqqney ha'am**], and he set before them [**vayyasem lifneyhem**] all these words [**et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh**] which the LORD had commanded him [**asher tsivvahu YHWH**].

**8** And all the people answered [**vayyaanu khol-ha'am**] together [**yachdav**] and said [**vayyo'meru**], "All [**kol**] that the LORD has spoken [**asher-dibber YHWH**] we will do [**na'aseh**]." And Moses brought back [**vayyashev Mosheh**] the words of the people [**et-divrey ha'am**] to the LORD [**el-YHWH**].

**9** And the LORD said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to Moses [**el-Mosheh**], "Behold, I am coming [**hinneh anokhi ba**] to you in the thickness of the cloud [**be'av he'anan**], so that the people may hear [**ba'avur yishma ha'am**] when I speak with you [**bedabberi immakh**], and also in you they may trust [**vegam-bekha ya'aminu**] forever [**le'olam**]." And Moses told [**vayyagged Mosheh**] the words of the people [**et-divrey ha'am**] to the LORD [**el-YHWH**].

---

## Instructions for Consecration (vv. 10–15)

**10** And the LORD said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to Moses [**el-Mosheh**], "Go [**lekh**] to the people [**el-ha'am**] and consecrate them [**veqiddashtam**] today and tomorrow [**hayyom umachar**], and let them wash [**vekibbsu**] their garments [**simlotam**].

**11** And let them be ready [**vehayu nekhonim**] for the third day [**layyom hashlishi**], for on the third day [**ki bayyom hashlishi**] the LORD will come down [**yered YHWH**] before the eyes of [**le'eyney**] all the people [**kol-ha'am**] upon Mount Sinai [**al-har Sinay**].

**12** And you shall set bounds [**vehigbalta**] for the people [**et-ha'am**] round about [**saviv**], saying [**le'mor**], 'Guard yourselves [**hishshamru lakhem**] from going up [**alot**] on the mountain [**bahar**] or touching [**unegoa**] its edge [**beqatsehu**]; anyone who touches [**kol-hanogea**] the mountain [**bahar**] shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**].

**13** No hand shall touch him [**lo-tigga bo yad**], for he shall surely be stoned [**ki-saqol yissaqel**] or surely be shot through [**o-yaroh yiyyareh**]; whether beast [**im-behemah**] or man [**im-ish**], he shall not live [**lo yichyeh**]. When the ram's horn [**bimshokh hayyovel**] sounds a long blast [**bimshokh**], they shall come up [**hemmah ya'alu**] to the mountain [**vahar**]."

**14** And Moses went down [**vayyered Mosheh**] from the mountain [**min-hahar**] to the people [**el-ha'am**], and he consecrated [**vayeqaddesh**] the people [**et-ha'am**], and they washed [**vayekabbsu**] their garments [**simlotam**].

**15** And he said [**vayyomer**] to the people [**el-ha'am**], "Be ready [**heyu nekhonim**] for the third day [**lishloshset yamim**]; do not go near [**al-tiggashu**] a woman [**el-ishah**]."

---

## The Theophany on the Third Day (vv. 16–20)

**16** And it came to pass [**vayehi**] on the third day [**vayyom hashlishi**], when it was morning [**bihyot habboqer**], that there were thunders [**vayyehi qolot**] and lightnings [**uveraqim**] and a heavy cloud [**ve'anan kaved**] upon the mountain [**al-hahar**], and the sound of a horn [**veqol shofar**] exceedingly strong [**chazaq me'od**]; and all the people [**vayyecherad kol-ha'am**] who were in the camp trembled [**asher bamachaneh**].

**17** And Moses brought out [**vayyotse Mosheh**] the people [**et-ha'am**] to meet [**liqra't**] God [**ha'Elohim**] from the camp [**min-hamachaneh**], and they stationed themselves [**vayyityatsvu**] at the foot of the mountain [**betachtit hahar**].

**18** And Mount Sinai [**vehar Sinay**] was all of it smoking [**ashan kullo**], because [**mippeney asher**] the LORD had descended [**yarad alav YHWH**] upon it in fire [**ba'esh**]; and its smoke went up [**vayya'al ashano**] like the smoke of a kiln [**ke'ashan hakkivshan**], and all the mountain [**vayyecherad kol-hahar**] trembled greatly [**me'od**].

**19** And the sound of the horn [**veqol hashshofar**] was going [**holekh**] and growing exceedingly strong [**vechazaq me'od**]; Moses would speak [**Mosheh yedabber**], and God [**veha'Elohim**] would answer him [**ya'annennu**] with a voice [**veqol**].

**20** And the LORD came down [**vayyered YHWH**] upon Mount Sinai [**al-har Sinay**], to the top of the mountain [**el-rosh hahar**]; and the LORD called [**vayyiqra YHWH**] Moses [**leMosheh**] to the top of the mountain [**el-rosh hahar**], and Moses went up [**vayyaal Mosheh**].

---

## The Warning Against Breaking Through (vv. 21–25)

**21** And the LORD said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to Moses [**el-Mosheh**], "Go down [**red**], warn [**ha'ed**] the people [**ba'am**], lest they break through [**pen-yehersu**] to the LORD [**el-YHWH**] to gaze [**lir'ot**], and many of them [**venaphal mimmennu**] fall [**rav**].

**22** And also [**vegam**] the priests [**hakkohanim**] who draw near [**hannigashim**] to the LORD [**el-YHWH**] — let them consecrate themselves [**yitqaddashu**], lest the LORD [**pen-yifrats bahem YHWH**] break out against them."

**23** And Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**] to the LORD [**el-YHWH**], "The people are not able [**lo-yukhal ha'am**] to go up [**la'alot**] to Mount Sinai [**el-har Sinay**], for You Yourself warned us [**ki-attah ha'edotah banu**], saying [**le'mor**], 'Set bounds [**hagbel**] about the mountain [**et-hahar**] and consecrate it [**veqiddashtoh**].'"

**24** And the LORD said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to him [**elav**], "Go [**lekh**], descend [**red**], and come up [**ve'alita**], you [**attah**] and Aaron [**ve'Aharon**] with you [**immakh**]; but the priests [**vehakohanim**] and the people [**veha'am**] — let them not break through [**al-yehersu**] to go up [**la'alot**] to the LORD [**el-YHWH**], lest He break out against them [**pen-yifrats bam**]."

**25** And Moses went down [**vayyered Mosheh**] to the people [**el-ha'am**] and said [**vayyomer**] to them [**alehem**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**segullah** (v. 5) — "treasured possession." The Hebrew segullah denotes a king's personal treasure, a specially valued property set apart from ordinary possessions. The word occurs only eight times in the Hebrew Bible (Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; 14:2; 26:18; 1 Chr 29:3; Ps 135:4; Mal 3:17; Eccl 2:8), and in every covenantal context it describes Israel's status as God's own treasured property among the nations. The term is not "chosen people" in an abstract sense but a concrete, proprietary claim: Israel belongs to God as a king's private treasury belongs to the king. The LXX renders it laos periousios ("a people that is one's own"), a phrase that enters the New Testament at Titus 2:14.

**mamlechet kohanim** (v. 6) — "kingdom of priests." This compound phrase is unparalleled in the Hebrew Bible and has generated extensive debate. The construct form mamlechet (from mamlakhah, "kingdom, reign, royal dominion") governs kohanim ("priests"). The phrase could mean: (a) a kingdom composed entirely of priests — every Israelite serves as priest; (b) a kingdom that serves a priestly function among the nations — Israel mediates between God and humanity; (c) a royal priesthood — a priesthood endowed with kingly dignity. The formal equivalence rendering preserves the ambiguity of the construct chain. The LXX renders it basileion hierateuma ("a royal priesthood"), which collapses the construct into an adjective-noun pair and eliminates the "kingdom" sense, a rendering adopted verbatim in 1 Peter 2:9.

**goy qadosh** (v. 6) — "holy nation." The use of goy rather than am is striking. Elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible, goy typically refers to foreign nations while am denotes Israel in its covenant relationship. Here, however, God uses goy for Israel — the generic term for "nation" — precisely to contrast Israel's holiness (qadosh, "set apart, consecrated") with the nations among whom Israel is one. Israel is a goy, a nation like other nations, but qadosh — separated, consecrated, devoted wholly to God. The LXX renders this as ethnos hagion ("a holy nation").

**al-kanfey nesharim** (v. 4) — "on wings of eagles." The nesher is likely the griffon vulture (Gyps fulvus), the largest soaring bird in the Levant, with a wingspan exceeding 2.5 meters. The image evokes Deuteronomy 32:11, where the nesher stirs up its nest, hovers over its young, spreads its wings, and carries them on its pinions. The metaphor here is not of flight as escape but of flight as intimate divine portage — God bore Israel upon His own wings, not merely permitting their departure from Egypt but carrying them personally.

**be'av he'anan** (v. 9) — "in the thickness of the cloud." The construct av ("thickness, density") describes not merely a cloud but a dense, impenetrable mass. The cloud serves a dual theological function: it reveals God's presence (He is there, speaking) while concealing God's form (He cannot be seen). The cloud is the medium of both communication and hiddenness.

### Grammatical Notes

**shamoa tishme'u** (v. 5) — The infinitive absolute (shamoa) before the finite verb (tishme'u) creates emphatic intensification: "if you will indeed hear / truly listen." This construction, common in covenant-stipulation language, underscores the conditionality of the segullah status. The treasured-possession relationship is not automatic but contingent upon obedient hearing.

**mot yumat** (v. 12) — The Hophal imperfect with infinitive absolute, the standard formula for the death penalty in biblical law. The passive voice (yumat, "he shall be caused to die") places the execution under communal obligation rather than personal vengeance. The next verse's specification — stoning or shooting with arrows — ensures execution without physical contact, since the trespasser on holy ground is himself contaminated by his violation.

**saqol yissaqel o yaroh yiyyareh** (v. 13) — Twin infinitive-absolute constructions for execution methods: "he shall surely be stoned or he shall surely be shot through." The two methods share a common logic: they accomplish death at a distance, so that the executioner does not touch the condemned, who has been rendered dangerous by his contact with the holy mountain.

**vayyichan ... vayyachanu** (v. 2) — A shift from the plural (vayyachanu, "they encamped") to the singular (vayyichan, "he/it encamped"). The classical commentaries note this shift: the plural describes the ordinary encampment of a multitude, but the singular describes Israel encamped "as one man with one heart" (Rashi, citing the Mekhilta). The formal equivalence rendering preserves this number shift ("they encamped ... Israel encamped").

**bimshokh hayyovel** (v. 13) — "when the ram's horn sounds a long blast." The yovel is the ram's horn (later the term for the Jubilee year, Lev 25:10), and mashakh means "to draw out, extend, prolong." The construction indicates a sustained blast — not the short staccato blasts (teru'ah) but a single, prolonged tone signaling the divine permission to approach. The people may ascend only when the horn sounds its long note.

### Structural Notes

The chapter divides into five movements: (1) arrival at Sinai (vv. 1-2); (2) the covenant proposal — the eagle-wing speech (vv. 3-6); (3) the people's acceptance and Moses' mediation (vv. 7-9); (4) consecration instructions and boundary-setting (vv. 10-15); (5) the theophany itself (vv. 16-25). This five-part structure creates a progressive narrowing of access: all Israel is present in the wilderness (vv. 1-2); all Israel hears the covenant proposal through Moses (vv. 3-8); the mountain is bounded and the people consecrated (vv. 10-15); and finally only Moses ascends while the people stand below in terror (vv. 16-25).

The narrative contains a striking pattern of ascent and descent by Moses: he goes up to God (v. 3), comes down to the elders (v. 7), reports the people's words to the LORD (v. 8), receives the cloud-appearance announcement (v. 9), goes down to consecrate the people (v. 14), and is summoned again to the summit (v. 20), only to be sent back down immediately with another warning (v. 21), and ascends yet again with Aaron (v. 24). This repeated vertical movement — up to God, down to the people, up again, down again — dramatizes Moses' unique role as mediator. He alone traverses the boundary between the holy summit and the consecrated-but-distant camp. The mountain becomes an axis mundi, and Moses is the living conduit along its vertical axis.

The abrupt ending of verse 25 — "And Moses went down to the people and said to them" — is one of the most discussed textual puzzles in Exodus. The verse ends without reporting what Moses said. The Masoretic text simply terminates the chapter here, and the next words (Exod 20:1) are "And God spoke all these words, saying." The effect is dramatic: Moses opens his mouth, and before we hear his words, God Himself speaks the Decalogue. The lacuna — what Moses said — is swallowed by the theophanic voice. Whatever Moses was about to say is superseded by the divine speech of chapter 20.

### Theological Notes

The phrase "all the earth is Mine" (ki-li kol-ha'arets, v. 5) is the theological ground for Israel's election. God's choice of Israel as segullah is not because God has jurisdiction only over Israel; rather, precisely because He owns all the earth and all its peoples, His choice of one nation as His treasured possession is a sovereign, gratuitous act. The universality of divine ownership is the precondition for the particularity of divine election. Israel is chosen not from among peoples unknown to God but from among peoples who all belong to Him.

The theophany of verses 16-19 employs every sensory register: sight (lightning, smoke, fire, thick cloud), sound (thunder, horn blasts, the voice of God), touch (the mountain trembling), and presumably smell (the smoke of a kiln). This multisensory assault is the text's way of communicating that the encounter with God overwhelms every human faculty simultaneously. The people tremble (vayyecherad, v. 16), and the mountain itself trembles (vayyecherad, v. 18) — the same verb applied to both, collapsing the distinction between the human response and the cosmic response. When God descends, everything shakes.

The tension between verses 12-13 and verse 13b is theologically significant. The same mountain that is death to touch (vv. 12-13a) will, at the sound of the yovel, become a mountain the people may ascend (v. 13b). Holiness is not a static condition of the mountain but a dynamic condition of divine presence. When God descends, the mountain is lethal to approach; when the horn sounds — signaling a shift in the divine economy — the mountain opens. The boundary is not geographic but theophanic: it follows the movement of God, not the contour of the terrain.

The double warning (vv. 21-24) — God sending Moses back down even after the boundary has already been set — communicates the overwhelming gravitational pull of the theophany. The danger is not merely that people might disobey a rule; it is that they might be drawn irresistibly toward the divine presence and perish. Even the priests — those whose office brings them near to God — must specially consecrate themselves (v. 22). The holiness of Sinai exceeds even the priestly right of approach.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 19 (pages 60-61) preserves the LXX version with several notable divergences from the MT. At v. 1, the LXX specifies tou mēnos tou tritou ("of the third month") for bachodesh hashlishi and adds tēs exodou autōn ek gēs Aiguptou ("of their exodus from the land of Egypt") — using the very word exodos that will give the book its Greek name. At v. 2, the LXX preserves the singular/plural distinction partially, rendering vayyichan-sham Yisra'el as kai parenebalen ekei Israēl ("and Israel encamped there"). At v. 3, the LXX renders leveyt Ya'aqov as tō oikō Iakōb ("to the house of Jacob"), preserving the Hebrew construct closely. At v. 4, kanfey nesharim becomes pterugōn aetōn ("wings of eagles"), using aetos — the eagle proper — rather than a vulture term. At v. 5, the critical segullah is rendered laos periousios ("a people that is one's own, a people of special possession"), a phrase with no classical Greek precedent — it appears to be a Septuagintal coinage designed to capture the Hebrew concept. The LXX adds laos ("people") where the Hebrew has only the abstract segullah, making explicit what the Hebrew leaves implicit: the treasure is a people. At v. 6, mamlechet kohanim becomes basileion hierateuma ("a royal priesthood"), a significant interpretive shift: the Hebrew "kingdom of priests" (a kingdom composed of priests) becomes "a royal priesthood" (a priesthood that is royal) — the grammatical relationship between the two nouns is reversed. The LXX also renders goy qadosh as ethnos hagion ("a holy nation"), using the standard Greek equivalent. At v. 9, be'av he'anan becomes en stulō nephelēs ("in a pillar of cloud"), assimilating the Sinai cloud to the pillar of cloud (ammud he'anan) of the wilderness journey — a significant interpretive move, since the Hebrew av ("thickness, density") is not the same as ammud ("pillar"). At v. 13, the LXX renders bimshokh hayyovel as hotan hai salpinges kai hai keratinai ("when the trumpets and the horns"), pluralizing and specifying the instruments, whereas the Hebrew has only the single yovel. At v. 16, qolot uveraqim becomes phōnai kai astrapai kai nephelē gnophodēs ("voices and lightnings and a dark cloud"), adding gnophodēs ("dark, murky") to characterize the cloud. At v. 18, the LXX renders ke'ashan hakkivshan as hōs kapnos kaminou ("like smoke of a furnace"), using kaminos rather than kivshan — the same word used in Genesis 19:28 for the destruction of Sodom. At v. 19, the LXX's rendering of God's response is noteworthy: veha'Elohim ya'annennu veqol becomes ho de theos apekrithē autō phōnē ("and God answered him with a voice"), using the aorist apekrithē where the Hebrew imperfect (ya'annennu) suggests ongoing, repeated action. At v. 25, the LXX extends the abrupt MT ending by adding kai eipen autois ("and he said to them"), followed immediately by the Decalogue — thus the chapter break in the LXX tradition does not create the same lacuna as in the MT. Throughout these Vaticanus pages, the text is written in the characteristic three-column continuous uncial script, with Exodus 19 beginning on the lower portion of page 60 (following Exodus 18) and continuing into the upper columns of page 61.

--- LOGOS
